---
title: "Bayesian Linear Regression"
engine: markdown
---

::: {.lm-hero}
[Chapter 2 · Regression Models]{.eyebrow}

# Bayesian Linear Regression

[Give the coefficients a prior and the same data returns not one line but a distribution over lines, with a closed-form Gaussian posterior that sampling methods only approximate.]{.dek}
:::

Ordinary least squares returns a single coefficient vector, the one that minimizes
squared error. Bayesian regression returns a [posterior]{.term}: a full distribution over
coefficients, formed by combining a [prior]{.term} (what we believed before seeing data)
with the likelihood (how the data weighs each candidate). When the prior on the
coefficients is Gaussian and the noise is Gaussian with known variance, that posterior is
itself Gaussian and available in closed form. This is the [conjugate]{.term} case, where
prior and posterior share a family, so updating is linear algebra rather than simulation.
It is also the analytic truth that the Markov chain Monte Carlo of the full notebook
approximates.

::: {.defbox}
[Gaussian Posterior (conjugate regression)]{.lbl}
[ &Sigma;<sub>n</sub> = (&Sigma;<sub>0</sub><sup>&#8722;1</sup> + &sigma;<sup>&#8722;2</sup>X&#8868;X)<sup>&#8722;1</sup> &nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; &mu;<sub>n</sub> = &Sigma;<sub>n</sub>(&Sigma;<sub>0</sub><sup>&#8722;1</sup>&mu;<sub>0</sub> + &sigma;<sup>&#8722;2</sup>X&#8868;y) ]{.math}
:::

Here $(\boldsymbol{\mu}_0, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_0)$ are the prior mean and covariance,
$\sigma^2$ is the known noise variance, and $(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_n)$
are the posterior mean and covariance. With a broad (vague) prior the
$\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_0^{-1}$ term nearly vanishes and $\boldsymbol{\mu}_n$ collapses onto
the OLS estimate. A tight prior pulls it toward $\boldsymbol{\mu}_0$. The next cell makes
that comparison concrete.

## The posterior mean against OLS

We generate thirty points from $y = 1 + 2x + \varepsilon$ with noise standard deviation
$0.5$, place a broad Gaussian prior on the intercept and slope, and read off the posterior
in one matrix expression. The posterior mean lands beside the OLS estimate, and the
posterior standard deviations report how sure we are of each coefficient, something OLS
delivers only through a separate calculation.

The two languages draw their noise from different random number generators, so their data
sets are not identical and the last digits differ. Both posteriors sit near the true
$(1, 2)$, and in each language the posterior mean tracks that language's OLS fit.

::: {.panel-tabset group="lang"}

## Python
```{pyodide}
import numpy as np
np.set_printoptions(precision=3, suppress=True)
np.random.seed(42)

# Synthetic data: y = 1 + 2x + noise, with known noise sd 0.5
true_theta = np.array([1.0, 2.0])
true_sigma = 0.5
n = 30
x = np.random.uniform(0, 3, n)
y = true_theta[0] + true_theta[1] * x + np.random.normal(0, true_sigma, n)

# Design matrix with an intercept column
X = np.column_stack([np.ones(n), x])

# Gaussian prior on (theta0, theta1): mean 0, broad covariance
prior_mean = np.array([0.0, 0.0])
prior_cov  = np.diag([10.0, 10.0])
noise_var  = true_sigma**2                 # treat sigma^2 as known

# Conjugate Gaussian posterior (closed form)
prior_prec = np.linalg.inv(prior_cov)
post_cov   = np.linalg.inv(prior_prec + (X.T @ X) / noise_var)
post_mean  = post_cov @ (prior_prec @ prior_mean + (X.T @ y) / noise_var)

# OLS estimate for comparison
ols = np.linalg.inv(X.T @ X) @ (X.T @ y)
post_sd = np.sqrt(np.diag(post_cov))

print("                 theta0    theta1")
print(f"posterior mean  {post_mean[0]:7.3f} {post_mean[1]:7.3f}")
print(f"OLS estimate    {ols[0]:7.3f} {ols[1]:7.3f}")
print(f"true value      {true_theta[0]:7.3f} {true_theta[1]:7.3f}")
print(f"\nposterior sd    {post_sd[0]:7.3f} {post_sd[1]:7.3f}")
```

## R
```{webr}
set.seed(42)

# Synthetic data: y = 1 + 2x + noise, with known noise sd 0.5
true_theta <- c(1.0, 2.0)
true_sigma <- 0.5
n <- 30
x <- runif(n, 0, 3)
y <- true_theta[1] + true_theta[2] * x + rnorm(n, 0, true_sigma)

# Design matrix with an intercept column
X <- cbind(1, x)

# Gaussian prior on (theta0, theta1): mean 0, broad covariance
prior_mean <- c(0, 0)
prior_cov  <- diag(c(10, 10))
noise_var  <- true_sigma^2                 # treat sigma^2 as known

# Conjugate Gaussian posterior (closed form)
prior_prec <- solve(prior_cov)
post_cov   <- solve(prior_prec + (t(X) %*% X) / noise_var)
post_mean  <- as.vector(post_cov %*% (prior_prec %*% prior_mean + (t(X) %*% y) / noise_var))

# OLS estimate for comparison
ols <- as.vector(solve(t(X) %*% X, t(X) %*% y))
post_sd <- sqrt(diag(post_cov))

cat("                 theta0    theta1\n")
cat(sprintf("posterior mean  %7.3f %7.3f\n", post_mean[1], post_mean[2]))
cat(sprintf("OLS estimate    %7.3f %7.3f\n", ols[1], ols[2]))
cat(sprintf("true value      %7.3f %7.3f\n", true_theta[1], true_theta[2]))
cat(sprintf("\nposterior sd    %7.3f %7.3f\n", post_sd[1], post_sd[2]))
```

:::

## Predictive uncertainty that widens away from the data

A point estimate predicts a single $y$ for each new $x$. The posterior predicts a
distribution. For an input $x^\*$ the [posterior predictive]{.term} variance is
$x^{\*\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_n x^\* + \sigma^2$: parameter uncertainty plus observation
noise. Plot the posterior mean line with its 95% [credible interval]{.term} and the band
tells an honest story. It is tightest where the data cluster and flares outward where the
model is extrapolating, because the parameter-uncertainty term grows as $x^\*$ moves from
the data center.

::: {.panel-tabset group="lang"}

## Python
```{pyodide}
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

np.random.seed(42)
true_theta = np.array([1.0, 2.0]); true_sigma = 0.5
n = 30
x = np.random.uniform(0, 3, n)
y = true_theta[0] + true_theta[1] * x + np.random.normal(0, true_sigma, n)
X = np.column_stack([np.ones(n), x])

prior_cov = np.diag([10.0, 10.0]); noise_var = true_sigma**2
prior_prec = np.linalg.inv(prior_cov)
post_cov  = np.linalg.inv(prior_prec + (X.T @ X) / noise_var)
post_mean = post_cov @ ((X.T @ y) / noise_var)        # prior mean is zero

# Posterior predictive over a grid: mean and variance at each x*
xs = np.linspace(-0.5, 3.5, 100)
Xs = np.column_stack([np.ones(len(xs)), xs])
pred_mean = Xs @ post_mean
pred_var  = np.sum((Xs @ post_cov) * Xs, axis=1) + noise_var
pred_sd   = np.sqrt(pred_var)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.fill_between(xs, pred_mean - 1.96 * pred_sd, pred_mean + 1.96 * pred_sd,
                color="#076FA1", alpha=0.20, label="95% credible band")
ax.plot(xs, pred_mean, color="#31417A", linewidth=2.5, label="posterior mean")
ax.plot(xs, true_theta[0] + true_theta[1] * xs, color="#666666", linestyle="--",
        linewidth=2, label="true line")
ax.scatter(x, y, s=50, color="#076FA1", edgecolor="white", zorder=5, label="data")
ax.set_xlabel("x"); ax.set_ylabel("y"); ax.legend(loc="upper left")
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

# The band is narrowest near the data center and widens outward
print(f"predictive sd at x=1.5 (center): {pred_sd[np.argmin(np.abs(xs - 1.5))]:.3f}")
print(f"predictive sd at x=3.5 (edge):   {pred_sd[-1]:.3f}")
```

## R
```{webr}
set.seed(42)
true_theta <- c(1.0, 2.0); true_sigma <- 0.5
n <- 30
x <- runif(n, 0, 3)
y <- true_theta[1] + true_theta[2] * x + rnorm(n, 0, true_sigma)
X <- cbind(1, x)

prior_cov <- diag(c(10, 10)); noise_var <- true_sigma^2
prior_prec <- solve(prior_cov)
post_cov  <- solve(prior_prec + (t(X) %*% X) / noise_var)
post_mean <- as.vector(post_cov %*% ((t(X) %*% y) / noise_var))   # prior mean is zero

# Posterior predictive over a grid: mean and variance at each x*
xs <- seq(-0.5, 3.5, length.out = 100)
Xs <- cbind(1, xs)
pred_mean <- as.vector(Xs %*% post_mean)
pred_var  <- rowSums((Xs %*% post_cov) * Xs) + noise_var
pred_sd   <- sqrt(pred_var)

plot(x, y, pch = 19, col = "#076FA1", xlim = c(-0.5, 3.5),
     xlab = "x", ylab = "y")
polygon(c(xs, rev(xs)),
        c(pred_mean - 1.96 * pred_sd, rev(pred_mean + 1.96 * pred_sd)),
        col = rgb(7, 111, 161, 50, maxColorValue = 255), border = NA)
lines(xs, pred_mean, col = "#31417A", lwd = 2.5)
lines(xs, true_theta[1] + true_theta[2] * xs, col = "#666666", lty = 2, lwd = 2)
points(x, y, pch = 19, col = "#076FA1")
legend("topleft", bty = "n",
       legend = c("data", "posterior mean", "true line", "95% band"),
       col = c("#076FA1", "#31417A", "#666666", "#076FA1"),
       pch = c(19, NA, NA, 15), lty = c(NA, 1, 2, NA))

cat(sprintf("predictive sd at x=1.5 (center): %.3f\n", pred_sd[which.min(abs(xs - 1.5))]))
cat(sprintf("predictive sd at x=3.5 (edge):   %.3f\n", pred_sd[length(pred_sd)]))
```

:::

## What the prior does

Prior and data jointly determine the posterior, and the balance between them is the whole
point of going Bayesian. Hold the data fixed and vary only the prior. A vague prior steps
aside and lets the data speak, recovering OLS. A tight prior centered on the truth sharpens
the estimate. A tight prior centered on the wrong values drags the posterior mean away,
though enough data eventually overrules it.

::: {.panel-tabset group="lang"}

## Python
```{pyodide}
import numpy as np

np.random.seed(42)
true_theta = np.array([1.0, 2.0]); true_sigma = 0.5
n = 30
x = np.random.uniform(0, 3, n)
y = true_theta[0] + true_theta[1] * x + np.random.normal(0, true_sigma, n)
X = np.column_stack([np.ones(n), x])
noise_var = true_sigma**2

def posterior_mean(prior_mean, prior_cov):
    P = np.linalg.inv(prior_cov)
    S = np.linalg.inv(P + (X.T @ X) / noise_var)
    return S @ (P @ prior_mean + (X.T @ y) / noise_var)

priors = [
    ("vague",             np.array([0.0,  0.0]), np.diag([100.0, 100.0])),
    ("informative right", np.array([1.0,  2.0]), np.diag([0.5, 0.5])),
    ("informative wrong", np.array([5.0, -1.0]), np.diag([0.5, 0.5])),
]

print(f"{'prior':<18} theta0   theta1")
for name, m0, S0 in priors:
    m = posterior_mean(m0, S0)
    print(f"{name:<18}{m[0]:6.3f}  {m[1]:6.3f}")
print(f"{'(truth)':<18}{true_theta[0]:6.3f}  {true_theta[1]:6.3f}")
```

## R
```{webr}
set.seed(42)
true_theta <- c(1.0, 2.0); true_sigma <- 0.5
n <- 30
x <- runif(n, 0, 3)
y <- true_theta[1] + true_theta[2] * x + rnorm(n, 0, true_sigma)
X <- cbind(1, x)
noise_var <- true_sigma^2

posterior_mean <- function(prior_mean, prior_cov) {
  P <- solve(prior_cov)
  S <- solve(P + (t(X) %*% X) / noise_var)
  as.vector(S %*% (P %*% prior_mean + (t(X) %*% y) / noise_var))
}

priors <- list(
  list("vague",             c(0,  0), diag(c(100, 100))),
  list("informative right", c(1,  2), diag(c(0.5, 0.5))),
  list("informative wrong", c(5, -1), diag(c(0.5, 0.5)))
)

cat(sprintf("%-18s theta0   theta1\n", "prior"))
for (p in priors) {
  m <- posterior_mean(p[[2]], p[[3]])
  cat(sprintf("%-18s%6.3f  %6.3f\n", p[[1]], m[1], m[2]))
}
cat(sprintf("%-18s%6.3f  %6.3f\n", "(truth)", true_theta[1], true_theta[2]))
```

:::

A Gaussian prior on the coefficients is the same object as the L2 penalty of ridge
regression. The posterior mean under that prior is exactly the ridge (MAP) estimate, with
the prior variance setting the inverse of the penalty strength. The Bayesian and the
regularized least-squares views meet here, a connection Chapter 7 returns to.

## When the posterior has no closed form

The Gaussian posterior above exists because a Gaussian prior is conjugate to a Gaussian
likelihood. Most models break that symmetry. Put a prior on the noise scale itself, swap
the Gaussian likelihood for a logistic or Poisson link, or stack a hierarchy of groups, and
the posterior integral loses its closed form. [Markov chain Monte Carlo]{.term} then draws
samples from the posterior instead of solving for it. The companion notebook runs the full
PyMC version, sampling this same regression with the NUTS sampler so you can check the draws
against the exact posterior computed above.

```{=html}
<a class="colab-btn" href="https://colab.research.google.com/" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span class="dot"></span> Open in Google Colab</a>
```

::: {.explore}
[Try it]{.lbl}
In the prior cell, shrink the wrong prior's covariance from `diag(0.5, 0.5)` to
`diag(0.01, 0.01)` and re-run: a confident wrong prior drags the posterior mean far from the
data. Then raise `n` from 30 to 300 and watch even that stubborn prior surrender as the
likelihood takes over.
:::
